title: Pitta bread
subtitle: Yeasted flatbreads baked at high heat to form a pocket
excerpt: Six pitta breads baked in a very hot oven until they puff into hollow pockets. Ready in under two hours including proving, they are best eaten warm from the oven.
sourceNotes: Pitta bread is one of the oldest documented leavened flatbreads, with archaeological evidence from ancient Egypt and the Levant. The basic formula of flour, water, yeast, and salt baked at high heat appears in Middle Eastern baking literature of the nineteenth century. Mrs Beeton's contemporaries noted the Arab flatbread tradition; the method here follows the straightforward home-oven approach documented in public-domain British baking manuals of the early twentieth century.

content.text: Pitta puffing relies on two things: a dough with enough structure to trap steam, and a very hot surface for the dough to land on. The sudden heat causes an

so rapid that the two layers of dough separate before either can dry out and seal. A preheated baking stone or heavy tray is the key piece of equipment; a cold tray put in with the dough will produce flat bread.

Combine the flour, yeast, and salt in a bowl. Add the oil and water and mix to a shaggy dough. Turn out and knead for 8 to 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. The dough should be slightly tacky but not sticky; if it clings to your hands, add a little more flour, a tablespoon at a time.

Return the dough to the bowl, cover, and leave at room temperature for 1 hour until doubled. Meanwhile, put a heavy baking tray or pizza stone in the oven and preheat to 250°C conventional (or as high as your oven will go).

Divide the dough into 6 equal pieces (about 85 g each). On a lightly floured surface, roll each piece to an oval roughly 20 cm long and 3 to 4 mm thick. Thinner pittas puff more readily; thicker ones are chewier. Cover the rolled pittas with a clean cloth and leave to rest for 15 minutes.

Slide two or three pittas directly onto the preheated stone or tray. Bake for 3 to 4 minutes. The pittas will puff fully within the first 2 minutes; they are done when the puffed dome has set and the surface shows a few pale gold patches. Do not over-bake; pitta that is dry and crisp will not fold without cracking. Remove with a fish slice and cover with a clean cloth while the remaining pittas bake.
Pittas not puffing: the oven or tray is not hot enough, or the dough is too thick. Ensure the tray preheats for at least 30 minutes and roll the dough to no more than 4 mm.
Pittas cracking when you open them: over-baked and dried out. Reduce the bake time by 30 seconds and cover immediately after removing from the oven.
